did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) equipped with a Softmax classifier for skin lesion classification. The methodology involves preprocessing dermoscopic images (scaling, normalization, data augmentation), feature extraction via CNN layers, and classification into seven skin cancer categories (e.g., melanoma, basal cell carcinoma) using the HAM10000 and ISIC datasets. The system is implemented as a web-based application for early skin cancer detection.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and implementation of an AI-based skin cancer detection system using a CNN with a Softmax classifier. It details a specific methodology including data preprocessing (scaling, normalization, augmentation), feature extraction, and classification on the HAM10000 and ISIC datasets. The authors report their own performance metrics (e.g., 85% accuracy for CNN, 88% for SVM) and describe the deployment of a web-based application, indicating it is an original technical study rather than a literature review.

title: Enhancing Early Skin Cancer Detection Through AI-Based Image Classification
brief_summary: This study presents an AI-based skin cancer detection system utilizing a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with a Softmax classifier to categorize skin lesions into seven distinct classes, including melanoma and basal cell carcinoma. The methodology involves preprocessing dermoscopic images through scaling, normalization, and data augmentation, followed by feature extraction and classification using publicly available datasets such as HAM10000 and ISIC. The system is deployed as a web-based application to provide quick, non-invasive, and accessible dermatological diagnosis for users. The approach aims to enhance early detection capabilities, particularly in underserved areas, by automating the identification of malignant versus benign lesions. The findings underscore the potential of deep learning to improve diagnostic accuracy and support telemedicine applications in dermatology.

all_task_types.target: Seven distinct skin lesion categories (benign keratosis-like lesions, basal cell carcinoma, melanoma, vascular lesions, dermatofibroma, melanocytic nevi, actinic keratoses)
all_task_types.details: The paper describes a multi-class classification task using a CNN with a Softmax classifier to categorize dermoscopic images into seven predefined skin cancer/lesion classes. The model outputs probability scores for each class and selects the most likely diagnosis. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and ROC-AUC.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and ROC-AUC.

HAM10000.notes: The CNN model achieved the highest accuracy of 92%, outperforming SVM (88%), KNN (85%), and LBPH (82%). The CNN also provided balanced precision-recall values. Specific precision, recall, and F1-score values for CNN are listed in Table I as 90%, 93%, and 91% respectively. SVM had Precision 87%, Recall 89%, and F1-Score 88%. LBPH had Precision 90%, Recall 93%, and F1-Score 91% (Note: Text in Chunk 11 lists LBPH metrics as Acc 92, Prec 90, Rec 93, F1 91, but Table I in Chunk 11 lists LBPH Acc 82. ISIC.notes: A CNN-based method on the 2,637-image ISIC dataset achieved a classification accuracy of 88% for identifying benign or malignant lesions. Another study cited (Sowmya Lekshmi [5]) used the ISIC dataset and obtained an accuracy of over 80% with CNN and 82% with SVM (using RBF kernel).

ISIC-ISBI 2016.notes: A Random Forest classifier obtained an accuracy of 93.89% on the ISIC-ISBI 2016 dataset after resolving class imbalance using SMOTE.

MoAA.1.notes: A low-cost, computationally effective CNN model for skin cancer classification using the HAM10000 dataset achieved an accuracy of 78%. Vascular lesions and basal cell carcinoma had the highest misclassification rates.

General Comparison (Table I).notes: CNN provided the maximum total accuracy and balanced precision-recall values, making it the most effective algorithm. CNN also achieved a ROC-AUC Score of 0.88.

conclusions: The proposed Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with a Softmax classifier achieved the highest accuracy of 92% on the HAM10000 dataset, outperforming Support Vector Machine (SVM) at 88%, K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) at 85%, and Local Binary Patterns Histogram (LBPH) at 82%.

The CNN model demonstrated balanced precision (90%) and recall (93%) with an F1-score of 91% and a ROC-AUC score of 0.88, making it the most effective algorithm for skin cancer classification among those tested.

The system was successfully deployed as a web-based application using Flask, providing a quick, non-invasive, and accessible tool for early skin cancer detection, particularly beneficial for underserved or remote areas.

Data preprocessing techniques, including scaling to 64x64 pixels, normalization, and data augmentation (flipping, rotation, contrast adjustment), were critical for improving model generalization and handling class imbalances common in medical datasets.

The study highlights the potential of deep learning to enhance diagnostic accuracy, support telemedicine applications, and reduce the workload for dermatologists by automating the identification of malignant versus benign lesions.
important_remarks: The study is a conference paper (ICOCT 2025) with limited scope and likely less rigorous peer review than high-impact journals.
The CNN architecture details are vague; specific layer counts, filter sizes, and kernel dimensions are not reported, only described as 'multiple' layers.
Input images were resized to a very small dimension (64x64 pixels), which may discard fine-grained diagnostic features critical for dermatological accuracy.
Data leakage risk: The paper uses HAM10000 and ISIC datasets but does not explicitly state if patient-level or image-level splits were used to prevent overlap between training and testing sets.
No external validation was performed; the model was only evaluated on the training/test splits of HAM10000 and ISIC, limiting generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.

Class imbalance issues are acknowledged but not quantitatively addressed in the primary CNN results; SMOTE is only mentioned in the context of a cited Random Forest study, not the authors' main CNN model.
Performance metrics for KNN and LBPH are incomplete in Table I (Precision, Recall, F1-Score marked as dashes), making full comparison difficult.
The web-based application deployment is a proof-of-concept; no clinical usability study, user acceptance testing, or regulatory compliance (e.g., FDA, CE) is reported.

The paper relies heavily on cited works for comparative context rather than providing a comprehensive benchmark against state-of-the-art models (e.g., ResNet, EfficientNet) in the same experimental setup.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) is reported for the performance differences between models.
